features.name: Centralized Vault Storage
features.description: Provides secure, centralized storage and rotation for passwords, API keys, and certificates.

features.name: Hardware Security Modules (HSM)
features.description: Generates and stores cryptographic keys inside FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated Hardware Security Modules.

features.name: Automated Key Rotation
features.description: Rotates cryptographic keys and secret versions automatically based on defined schedules.

iamRoles.description: Full access to create, modify, and rotate cryptographic keys and key rings.
Permissions to create, modify, access, and rotate secrets inside Secret Manager.

cliExamples.description: Create a new secret in Secret Manager
Add a new secret version with static text
cliExamples.command: echo -n "super-secret-password" | gcloud secrets versions add secret-manager-secret --data-file=-

design: Secret Manager is chosen to store API keys, database credentials, and certificates securely.
Cloud Key Management Service (KMS) manages cryptographic keys used to encrypt data.
Envelope encryption: Cloud KMS manages Key Encryption Keys (KEK) that encrypt Data Encryption Keys (DEKs) which encrypt actual data.
Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) give you control over key rotation and access policies in GCP services.
Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK) are keys generated on-premises and provided to GCP during API calls.
KMS HSM keys generate and store cryptographic keys inside FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated physical modules.
Cloud External Key Manager (EKM) stores cryptographic keys outside of Google's network for complete sovereignty.
Secrets and keys are global or regional resources; choose regional storage to comply with data sovereignty regulations.
security: Do not store plaintext passwords in source repositories or container images; pull them from Secret Manager.
Manage access to secrets using roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor, avoiding administrative roles.
KMS key ring permissions must be configured at the key ring or key level, not the project level (least privilege).
Enable automated rotation policies for KMS keys and secrets to mitigate credential compromise risk.
Secret Manager allows configuring VPC Service Controls perimeters to prevent unauthorized secret fetching.
Audit logs track key rotations, key access events, and secret version retrievals.
Disable or destroy compromised KMS key versions immediately to prevent further decryption.
Always encrypt database connection passwords when mounting them to serverless containers.
reliability: KMS and Secret Manager replicate keys and secrets across multiple zones or regions.
Choose automatic replication for Secret Manager to distribute secrets globally for high availability.
Destroyed KMS keys have a 24-hour grace period during which they can be recovered before permanent deletion.
KMS key rings cannot be deleted, preventing accidental loss of keys encrypting legacy archives.
Secret versions are immutable; updates create a new version, allowing safe rollbacks.
KMS API endpoints are highly optimized to ensure decryption calls do not introduce app latency.
System alerts on key expiration or decryption errors are sent to Cloud Monitoring.
Configure backup decryption keys in recovery plans for disaster recovery.
cost: KMS charges per active key version per month and for cryptographic operations performed.
Secret Manager bills per active secret, per secret version, and for API access operations.
Disable or destroy unused KMS key versions to avoid monthly key storage fees.
Secret Manager charges for API operations; cache retrieved secrets in application memory to minimize API billing.
Consolidate keys on shared Key Rings where possible to simplify billing structures.
Avoid using EKM (External Key Manager) for high-frequency operations due to network egress costs.
Take advantage of free tier limits for secret lookups and KMS decryption operations.
Monitor decryption API call rates using billing dashboards to detect inefficient application code.
